params.text:
Assessing the risk: TILE

When a handling task cannot be avoided, it must be assessed. Care staff often use the memory aid TILE (sometimes written LITE) to think through the risks before they act:

  • T – Task: what does the move involve? Does it require twisting, stooping, reaching, holding a position or repeating the move often?
  • I – Individual: your own capability — your training, fitness, health, and whether you are pregnant or have an injury.
  • L – Load: the person or object being moved — their weight, ability to help, cooperation, pain, or medical devices such as a catheter.
  • E – Environment: the space around you — floor surface, clutter, lighting, room to move and where the equipment is.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Explore each part of the TILE assessment. Tap a card to reveal what to check.

dialogs.answer: What does the move involve? Watch for twisting, stooping, reaching, holding awkward positions, long distances or frequent repetition.
Are you fit and trained for this move? Consider your own health, strength, any injury or pregnancy, and whether you need a second person.
For a person, think about their weight, how much they can help, their cooperation, pain, skin condition and any tubes or devices attached.
Is there enough space? Check the floor, clutter, lighting, temperature and whether the right equipment is close to hand.
It gives you a quick, structured pause before any move so nothing important is missed — a safe move can still go wrong in an unsafe environment.
